coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of few-layered and edge-abundant MoS2/C nanocomposites with enhanced electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Lin
Zhou, Xiaoping
Xu, Xuyao
Xu, Limei
Zhang, Lingling
Chen, Weixiang
coredata.dc:description: MoS2/C nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method with citrate ammonium as carbon source. The obtained samples are well characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Raman spectroscopy, thermo gravimetric analyzer (TG) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Moreover, they are also evaluated as electrochemical catalysts in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The results indicate that numerous ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets which are characteristic of few layers (1–3 layers) and abundant exposed active edges and defects are well-dispersed in the amorphous carbon network to form MoS2/C composite. As a consequence, the desirable microstructures endow the resultant MoS2/C composite with significantly enhanced catalytic activity for HER such as more positive onset potential, larger current density as well as smaller Tafel slope of 64mVdec− 1 in comparison with the bare MoS2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Chalcogenides
A. Composites
B. Chemical synthesis
D. Catalytic properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2/C nanocomposite
Microstructure and morphology of MoS2/C composite

item-info.ce:copyright: The Society of Powder Technology Japan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared (a) bare MoS2 and (b) MoS2/C composite.
TG curves of the MoS2/C composite.
Raman spectra of the as-prepared (a) bare MoS2 and (b) MoS2/C composite.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the as-prepared MoS2/C composite.
SEM, TEM and HRTEM images of the as-prepared (a and b) bare MoS2 and (c–f) MoS2/C composite.
Schematic illustration of the formation process of MoS2/C composite via a one-step hydrothermal route.
(a) Polarization curves, (b) corresponding Tafel plots, (c) a durability test of the MoS2/C composite electrode and (d) cyclic voltammograms recorded in 0.5M H2SO4 at a scan rate of 0.05Vs− 1.
Nyquist plots of the bare MoS2 and MoS2/C composite electrodes obtained by applying a sine wave with amplitude of 5.0mV over the frequency range from 106 Hz to 0.01Hz.
Elemental composition of the MoS2/C composites. S:Mo represents the atomic ratio.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of few-layered and edge-abundant MoS2/C nanocomposites with enhanced electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Physical Chemistry, Development Center for New Materials Engineering & Technology in Universities of Guangdong, Lingnan Normal University, Zhanjiang 524048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Development Center for New Materials Engineering & Technology in Universities of Guangdong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MoS2/C nanocomposite has been successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method with citrate ammonium as carbon source. The obtained samples are well characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Raman spectroscopy, thermo gravimetric analyzer (TG) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Moreover, they are also evaluated as electrochemical catalysts in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The results indicate that numerous ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets which are characteristic of few layers (1–3 layers) and abundant exposed active edges and defects are well-dispersed in the amorphous carbon network to form MoS2/C composite. As a consequence, the desirable microstructures endow the resultant MoS2/C composite with significantly enhanced catalytic activity for HER such as more positive onset potential, larger current density as well as smaller Tafel slope of 64mVdec− 1 in comparison with the bare MoS2.

ce:section.ce:para: Depletion of fossil fuels and increased environmental concerns have prompted an urgent search for clean and sustainable alternative energy sources [1]. The electrochemical splitting of water via proton reduction in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) provides a feasible route toward this without dependence on consumable resources. Electrocatalyst always plays an indispensable role in reducing the overpotential of electrodes in HER, consequently promoting the reaction yield and thus the energy storage efficacy [2,3]. To date, the best known catalysts for HER are precious metals, such as platinum, ruthenium, and iridium, but the scarcity and high cost of these materials prohibit their wide-scale deployment [4]. It is desirable but rather challenging to develop a large-scale, environmentally friendly synthesis of inexpensive alternatives with high electrocatalytic activity to replace Pt-based catalysts.

Molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) has a layered structure consisting of S–Mo–S arrangements connected to each sheet by van der Waals forces, which is a long-known catalyst for hydrodesulfurization [5,6]. Recent studies have shown that nanostructured MoS2 is a promising electrocatalyst for HER, while the catalytic activity of its bulk counterpart is poor [7,8]. Theoretical calculations combined with experiments have revealed that the edge sites of MoS2, especially the sulfur edge-terminated sites are identified as active sites for HER, on which the theoretical free energy of adsorption of hydrogen is similar to that of Pt, while the basal surfaces are catalytically inert [9,10]. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that the exchange current density of MoS2 nanocrystals is proportional to the length of edge sites [9,10]. For this reason, great efforts have been unremittingly made to enlarge the edge sites of MoS2 and various MoS2 nanostructures with preferentially exposed edge sites such as doublegyroid MoS2 [11], vertically aligned MoS2 layers [12,13], porous films [14,15], ultrathin MoS2 nanoplates [16,17], nanospheres [18,19], nanoflowers [20–23], highly divided or distorted MoS2 [24,25], optimized and tailored MoS2 nanosheets [26–30] as well as amorphous molybdenum sulfide and clusters [31,32] have been successfully prepared. However, the edge sites are highly energetic and unstable compared with basal plane. Therefore, the design and synthesis of nano-sized edge-terminated MoS2 are quite difficult and still remain a critical issue for application of MoS2 as a catalyst for HER [33,34].

Another pivotal inherent problem persecuting MoS2 in HER is originated from its poor conductivity, especially the extremely low conductivity between two adjacent van der Waals bonded S–Mo–S layers, which greatly compromises the catalytic performance [35]. It is confirmed that the conductivity along two vertically stacked S–Mo–S interlayers is about 2 orders of magnitude lower than that of the intralayers [36]. In addition, the stacking of MoS2 layers would severely decrease the amount of exposed active sites [37]. Fortunately, layered features along with the weak interlayer interaction has provided the potential capability of exfoliation of bulk MoS2 into graphene-like single-layered (SL-) or few-layered (FL-) nanosheets through mechanical or chemical techniques [38]. Apparently, reduction of the layers is favorable to decreasing the interlayered resistance and improving the hopping efficiency of electrons between MoS2 layers [35]. To the present, graphene-like MoS2 nanosheets has gradually become a very competitive alternative electrocatalyst in HER [17,39–41]. Another approach toward improving the electrical contact is to couple MoS2 with other conductive materials such as carbon nanotubes, pyrolyzed carbon as well as graphene to fabricate engineered composites [12,17,20,42–48]. It has been demonstrated that incorporation of these conductive materials can effectively promote electron transport from the active sites to the electrodes, accelerate the kinetics of electrode reaction and thus enhance the HER catalytic activity [12,17,20].

Herein, we present a one-step hydrothermal approach to synthesize MoS2/C nanocomposite with citrate ammonium as carbon source and appraise them as an electrocatalyst for HER activity. The samples are characterized by XRD, SEM, EDX, TEM/HRTEM, Raman, TG and XPS techniques. The experimental results indicate that the as-prepared MoS2/C composite is composed of numerous few-layered and edge-abundant ultrathin MoS2 nanosheets dispersed in amorphous carbon. As a result, the as-prepared MoS2/C nanocomposite demonstrates prominently enhanced electrocatalytic performance for hydrogen evolution reaction compared with the bare MoS2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2/C nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthetic procedure, 1.5mmol of ammonium citrate were dissolved into 50mL deionized water to form a solution. Then 1.5mmol of ammonium tetrathiomolybdate (NH4)2MoS4 were dissolved to the above solution with constant stirring. After that, 10ml of hydrazine hydrate solution was dropwise added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. Afterwards, the solution was transferred into the 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated and maintained at 240°C for 24h. After cooling naturally, the black solid product was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried in the vacuum oven at 80°C for 12h. In control experiment, the bare MoS2 sample was also prepared by a similar synthetic route without ammonium citrate in hydrothermal solution.

XRD patterns were recorded with a Thermo X’TRA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =0.154056nm). The morphologies of the samples were observed by using a SIRION-100 field emission SEM (FESEM). The elemental composition of the samples was analyzed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, GENENIS-4000) and thermogravimetric analysis (TG), which was carried out with a NETZSCH STA 409 PC apparatus at a heating rate of 10°Cmin− 1 in flowing air. TEM and HRTEM characterizations were performed on a JEOL JFL-2010 TEM operating at 200kV, for which each sample was prepared by dispersing it in acetone and drop-casting onto a 200 mesh copper grid coated with holey carbon. Raman spectra were recorded with 514nm Ar ion laser at 6mW for 50s employing a Jobin Yvon LabRam HR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were processed on a Perkin–Elmer PHI5000c XPS, using C1s (B.E. 284.6eV) as a reference.

Electrochemical measurements were performed in a three-electrode system at an electrochemical station (CHI660D). Typically, 4mg of catalyst and 80μL Nafion solution (5wt%) were dispersed in 1mL water–ethanol solution with volume ratio of 4:1 by sonicating for 1h to form a homogeneous ink. Then 5μL of the dispersion (containing 20μg of catalyst) was loaded onto a glassy carbon electrode with 5mm diameter (loading ca. 0.102mgcm−2). Linear sweep voltammetry with scan rate of 5mVs− 1 was conducted in 0.5M H2SO4 (purged with pure N2) using saturated calomel as the reference electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and the glassy carbon electrode with various samples as the working electrode. All the potentials were calibrated to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Number of active sites was estimated according to the methods reported previously [16]. AC impedance measurements were carried out in the same configuration at η =200mV from 106 to 0.01Hz with an AC voltage of 5mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and morphology of MoS2/C composite
ce:section.ce:para: The crystalline structure and crystallinity of the as-prepared materials are inspected by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the results are plotted in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1 shows that the pattern of the bare MoS2 agrees well with a hexagonal structure (JCPDS 37-1492) which belongs to the space group P63 /mmc. The peaks at 2θ =13.9°, 33.7°, 57.6° and 69.2° are attributed to the (002), (100), (110) and (201) planes, respectively. The appearance of the (002) peak signifies a stacked layered structure though the weak diffraction peaks are indicative of the low crystallinity of the bare MoS2. In sharp contrast to that of the bare MoS2, the (002) plane peak of the MoS2/C composite become extremely weak and obscure, indicating that the stacking of MoS2 layers has been significantly inhibited which was also observed in previous work [44,47,48]. Thus we can speculate that the carbonaceous materials produced by the hydrothermal carbonization of citrate ammonium could not only improve the electronic conductivity, but also insert into the MoS2 layers to prevent them from stacking. Finally, a poorly stacked and few-layered MoS2 nanosheets are formed in the composite [44,47,48].

EDX was adopted to identify the elemental compositions of the MoS2/C composites. The results are summarized in Table 1 . Table 1 shows that the composites contain C, O, N, Mo and S. The calculated atomic ratio of S–Mo element is 2.03, approaching the theoretical stoichiometric value of MoS2. The C, O and N element are supplied by amorphous carbon stemming from the polymerization, dehydration and carbonization reactions of the citrate ammonium during the hydrothermal process. The mass fraction of MoS2 in the composites can be ascertained to be around 65.34%. To further determine the content of MoS2 in composite, the composite was also characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TG). Fig. 2 exhibits a small weight loss below 200°C due to the vaporization of adsorbed water and a large continuous weight loss in the range of approximately 300–500°C which was caused by the oxidation of MoS2 and amorphous carbon. Based on the fact that the final residue is MoO3 after complete oxidation, the mass fraction of MoS2 in the composites can be ascertained to be around 64.92% which is in good agreement with the result of EDX.

The samples are further investigated by Raman spectra as shown in Fig. 3 . The spectra of both samples illustrate the characteristic peaks at 378.5, 377.8cm− 1 (E1 2g mode) and 403.6, 401.5cm− 1 (A1g mode), respectively for the bare MoS2 and MoS2/C composites. The E1 2g mode peak involves the in-layer displacements of Mo and S atoms, whereas the A1g mode peak involves the out-of-layer symmetric displacements of S atoms along the c-axis [49]. It is noticed that the intensities of E1 2g and A1g peaks of the MoS2 in composites are visibly lower than those of the pristine MoS2. The weakened E1 2g and A1g modes could be indicative of substantial defect sites in the crystal structure of MoS2 in composites, which can act as active edge sites and contribute a lot to the enhanced HER activity [44]. Moreover, it can be discerned that MoS2/C nanocomposites have a down-shift of A1g peak in comparison with the bare MoS2. It has been reported that the monolayer or few-layer MoS2 crystal has a down-shift of A1g peak with the decreasing layer number [50]. The frequency shift of the A1g peaks further confirms the few-layer feature of MoS2 in composites. In addition, the appearance of E1 1g, LA and 2LA(M) is associated with the vibration of one MoS2 layer against neighboring layers and is called the rigid layer mode [49,50].

The chemical states of Mo, S and C in the as-prepared MoS2/C were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The high resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 4 a) show that the C1s core level can be deconvoluted into two peaks. The main peak at 284.6eV is assigned to an sp2 hybridized graphite-like carbon atom and the peak around 288.2eV is attributed to the carbon atom bound to one oxygen atom [44,47,48]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the strongest Mo signal arises from 3d5/2 =229.0eV characteristic of a +4 oxidation state [44,47,48]. This is further confirmed by the presence of a single doublet in the S2p region (Fig. 4c) at lower binding energies, which corresponds to the S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbitals of divalent sulfide ions (S2 −) at 161.90 and 163.11eV, respectively. In addition, the S 2p1/2 energy at 164.2eV suggests the existence of bridging S2 2 −. The presence of the higher energy peak indicate that the active HER sites are likely to be related to these species [44,47,48]. Additionally, the calculated atomic ratio of S to Mo element from XPS is 2.08, close to the result of EDX.

The morphology and microstructure of the obtained samples are further investigated by SEM and TEM images. Fig. 5 a provides a full view of the bare MoS2, in which conglobatus particles with irregular shapes and coarse surfaces can be found. The inserted TEM figure on the right bottom of Fig. 5a shows the profile of a separated particle, from which it can be observed that many curved nanosheets stretch out of the edge of the particle, suggesting that it is composed of assembled nanosheets. Fig. 5b clearly displays the densely and straightly arranged crystal fringes of the bare MoS2 nanosheets with about 10 stacking layers and an interlayer spacing of 0.65nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of 2H–MoS2. By contrast, the MoS2/C composite exhibits an interconnected spherical morphology with diameter of 380nm as shown in Fig. 5c. It can also be found that the MoS2/C composite is also comprised of wrinkled nanosheets. Close observation from Fig. 5d reveals that the MoS2 nanosheets are evenly distributed in the composite and possess the few-layered characteristic (1–3 layers) as well as rich exposed edges, which is in good accordance with the analysis of XRD and Raman. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5e and f, some crystal fringes along the curled edges of MoS2 nanosheets are discontinuous, which originates from the existence of abundant defects. It is speculated that relatively low reaction temperature and the introduction of amorphous carbon could limit the crystal growth of MoS2 and result in low crystallinity and defects [28,44,48]. Such a defect-rich structure can enormously increase the exposure of active edge sites owing to the formation of cracks on the basal surface brought by abundant defects [28].

The formation mechanism of few-layered and edge-abundant MoS2/C composite is illustrated in Fig. 6 . First, it is supposed that the ammonium citrate could exert an electrostatic interaction with MoS4 2 − ions in solution. During the hydrothermal process, MoS2 nanosheets could be produced by the redox reaction between MoS4 2 − ions and N2H2. Meanwhile, the ammonium citrate could be polymerized due to the inter-molecular H-bonding and then dehydrated and carbonized to form amorphous carbon materials [51,52]. After that, a number of carbon materials might insert into the van der Waals interlaminar spaces of MoS2 to prevent MoS2 layers from stacking. Finally, the resultant poorly-stacked few-layered MoS2 nanosheets are dispersed in carbon materials and have more exposed edges and defects.

To demonstrate electrochemical performance of the MoS2/C composite for HER, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) is performed. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the polarization curves were obtained in 0.5M H2SO4 with a scan rate of 5mVs− 1. For comparison, polarization curves of the bare glass carbon electrode (GCE), bare MoS2, and Pt are also plotted in Fig. 7a. It is apparent from Fig. 7a that the glassy carbon substrate can be considered inert and its HER activity is negligible. Pt is known to have excellent HER catalytic performance and, as expected, a near-zero overpotential is observed. Note that the MoS2/C composite demonstrates much better catalytic activity with a more positive onset potential of 122mV than the bare MoS2 (143mV). The current density of the MoS2/C composite at 300mV is estimated to be 14.3mAcm− 2, which is nearly 10 times larger than that of the bare MoS2 (1.42mAcm− 2).

Further information about the HER activity of the various samples was acquired by analyzing the linear region of the Tafel plots as shown in Fig. 7b, which are fit well to the Tafel equation η = blog j + a, where j is the current density and b is the Tafel slope. It is known that the HER at a Pt electrode proceeds via the so-called Volmer–Tafel mechanism with a Tafel slope of 30mVdec− 1 [2]. This is reflected in our experimental data where the Pt electrode exhibited Tafel slope of 33mVdec− 1. Among the samples investigated in this work, the MoS2/C composite displays a much lower Tafel slope of 64mVdec− 1 than that of the bare MoS2 with the greater Tafel slope 185mVdec− 1, which means that the MoS2/C composite possesses a superior catalytic performance and a faster growth of the HER rate with increased potentials. Three possible reaction steps have been proposed for the HER in acidic solutions [2,7]. First is a primary discharge step (Volmer reaction):
(1)
H
3
O
+
+
e
-
→
H
ads
+
H
2
O

This step is followed by either an electrochemical desorption step (Heyrovsky reaction):
(2)
H
ads
+
H
3
O
+
+
e
-
→
H
2
+
H
2
O
or a recombination step (Heyrovsky reaction):
(3)
H
ads
+
H
ads
→
H
2

The Tafel slope is an inherent property of the catalyst that is determined by the rate-limiting step of the HER. The determination and interpretation of the Tafel slope are important for elucidation of the elementary steps involved. Generally, a fast discharge reaction (1) followed by a ratelimiting combination reaction (3) leads to a Tafel slope of 30mVdec− 1. While (1) is fast and is followed by a slow electrochemical desorption (2), a Tafel slope of 40mVdec− 1 is obtained. Moreover, if (1) is rate-limiting, the Tafel slope is 120mVdec− 1. Therefore, the HER on a Pt surface is followed by a combination of reactions (1) and (2). Unfortunately, the reaction mechanism on molybdenum sulfide catalysts remains inconclusive because of the discrepancy of the wide range of HER Tafel slopes reported since its first HER study more than 40years ago [9,10]. Even though these values can be adopted as a guide in identifying the HER mechanisms, it should be pointed out that these calculations are based on a strict set of assumptions that do not universally hold. As a result, identifying the HER mechanism according to the Tafel slope analysis alone is not always clear. Herein, it only can be concluded that a ratelimiting discharge step is involved in the HER behavior of the bare MoS2 and the mechanism for the MoS2/C composite is a fast discharge reaction (1) followed by either a rate-limiting Heyrovsky reaction or Tafel reaction because these two possible mechanisms cannot be distinguished completely at all.

Furthermore, to probe HER stability of the MoS2/C composite, we cycled the catalyst continuously for 500 cycles. As shown in Fig. 7c, at the end of cycling, the current density of the MoS2/C composite catalyst decreases slightly to 95% of the initial value, indicating that the MoS2/C composite catalyst exhibits excellent HER activity with reasonable long-term stability. To further clarify the reason for the enhanced HER catalytic property of the MoS2/C composite, the number of active edges is then roughly estimated through an electrochemical approach based on the previous work using following equation [16]:
(4)
n = Q / 2 Fm = I Δ V / 2 Fvm
where n is the number of active edges (mmol/g); Q (C) is the absolute voltammetric charges (including cathodic and anodic), which can be obtained from CV curves plotted in Fig. 7d; F is the Faraday constant (C/mol); m is the loading mass of catalyst on GCE (g); I is the current (mA); ΔV is the potential window (V); and v is the scanning rate (V/s). The calculated active edge sites of the MoS2/C composites is about 0.27mmolg− 1, which is much larger than that of the bare MoS2 (0.03mmolg− 1) and provides the direct evidence of the abundant active edge sites of the MoS2/C composite.

Fig. 8 shows the corresponding electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of the bare MoS2 and MoS2/C electrocatalysts, in order to further investigate the interface reactions and electrode kinetics in the HER. It is observed from the Nyquist plots that the diameter of the semicircle for MoS2/C is remarkably reduced compared with that of the bare MoS2, indicating the greatly decreased charge-transfer resistance due to the few-layered features and combination with conductive amorphous carbon.

In summary, the MoS2/C composite has been successfully synthesized by a one-pot facile hydrothermal method with citrate ammonium as carbon source and is evaluated as an electrochemical catalyst for HER. It is found out that the MoS2/C composite is comprised with few-layered MoS2 nanosheets with more exposed edges as well as amorphous carbon derived from the citrate ammonium. Electrochemical tests indicate that compared with the bare MoS2, the obtained MoS2/C composite demonstrates an obviously enhanced catalytic activity for HER such as more positive onset potential, larger current density and smaller Tafel slope of 64mVdec− 1. The achieved superior electrochemical performances can be attributed to the few-layered structural features and more exposed active edges of the MoS2 nanosheets as well as the combination with conductive amorphous carbon.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation for Joint Training Innovative Talents in East-West-North Guangdong (2014A030307030), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2014A010106032), the Strong Innovation School of Engineering Program of Department of Education of Guangdong Province (Distinctive Innovation Project No. 2014KTSCX157), Natural Science Foundation of Lingnan Normal University (LZL1402), Distinguished Young Talents Foundation in Higher Education of Guangdong (2013LYM_0054), the International Science and Technology Cooperation Program of China (2012DFG42100), and the Natural Science Foundation of China (21173190 and 21473156).
